sound_type: mournful oo-woo-woo -- soft, iconic five-note cooing 'oo-WOO-woo-woo-woo' at 350-550 Hz plus distinctive wing whistle on takeoff (second mechanical sound in the engine); E=3
ecosystem: Open woodlands, suburban areas, agricultural land, parks, gardens. One of the most adaptable and widespread birds in North America. Population 300-400 million. Found in every U.S. state. Thrives in human-modified landscapes.
season: Year-round caller. Peak breeding March-September. Multiple broods (2-6 per year). The most prolific breeder among NA Columbidae.
family: Columbidae (2nd entry. SECOND COLUMBIDAE. Native pigeon contrast pair with Band-tailed Pigeon at 149.)

coordinates.description: Mourning Doves are ubiquitous in the PNW -- suburban power lines, parks, agricultural areas, and open woodlands throughout the region.

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND Columbidae entry. Native pigeon contrast pair with Band-tailed Pigeon (149): forest vs. open, declining vs. thriving, hidden vs. ubiquitous. Both E=3. Dual-sound species: vocal coo + mechanical wing whistle. Second mechanical sound in the engine (after Common Goldeneye at 124).
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Columbidae: second entry. Band-tailed (149) + Mourning Dove (150).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Zenaida from Zenaide Bonaparte (wife of ornithologist Charles Lucien Bonaparte). macroura from Greek 'makros' (long) + 'oura' (tail) -- referring to the long, pointed tail.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Zenaida -- first entry. The 'typical doves' -- widespread, adaptable.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Second Columbidae. Native contrast pair: Band-tailed (forest, declining, 149) vs. Mourning Dove (open, thriving, 150).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Coo: 350-550 Hz. Harmonics to 2000 Hz. Low fundamental gives the 'mourning' quality.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Cooing: 350-550 Hz. Wing whistle: 1000-3000 Hz. Two distinct frequency ranges.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Coo phrase: 2.5-3.5 seconds (five notes). Wing whistle: 0.5-1.5 seconds.
song_metrics.note_structure: FIVE-NOTE COO: 'oo-WOO-woo-woo-woo' -- second note highest and most emphatic, then descending. Invariable pattern. Diagnostic.
song_metrics.note_types: Coo (primary, territorial/courtship), wing whistle (secondary, alarm/takeoff -- mechanical, 9th primary feather).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Five-note phrase repeated with 3-8 second pauses. Males call from exposed high perches. Year-round with peak in breeding season.
song_metrics.timbre: Soft, breathy, mournful. The drooping pitch contour and minor-key resonance create the perception of sadness. Named for its sound.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Soft. Audible 50-150m in still conditions. Background ambient. Heard by nearly everyone, noticed by almost no one.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate. 3-8 second pauses between five-note phrases. Extended calling bouts during breeding season dawn.
song_metrics.context: Males call from high exposed perches -- power lines, dead branches, rooftops. Year-round, peak March-September. Wing whistle on alarm takeoff.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Five-note pattern remarkably consistent across entire range.
song_metrics.key_property: MOURNFUL OO-WOO-WOO -- 350-550 Hz, five notes with second emphasized, the most recognized bird call in North America. PLUS wing whistle on takeoff (2nd mechanical sound). E=3. SECOND COLUMBIDAE. America's most hunted game bird (20M/year). Dual-sound species. Named for its call.

plumage.overall: Warm brown overall. Spotted wings. Long, pointed, white-edged tail (diagnostic). Slim, elegant build. Smaller than Collared-Dove and Band-tailed.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Long pointed tail with white edges. Slim elegant build. Warm brown. Spots on wings. No collar.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct. Distinctive wing whistle on takeoff. Pointed tail visible in flight.

physical_description.overall: Length: 23-34 cm. Weight: 100-170 g. Slim, elegant build. Smaller than Eurasian Collared-Dove (150-240g) and much smaller than Band-tailed Pigeon (300-450g).
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Long pointed tail. Slim build. Warm brown plumage. Wing whistle on takeoff. Ubiquitous on power lines.
physical_description.field_id: Slim, warm-brown dove on power lines, fences, or rooftops. Long pointed tail. Listen for the mournful five-note coo and the sharp wing whistle on takeoff.

nodes.title: Most Recognized Bird Call in America
nodes.description: The five-note 'oo-WOO-woo-woo-woo' is embedded in American cultural memory. Often mistaken for an owl. The sound of summer mornings, rural landscapes, suburban dawn. Named for its mournful quality.

nodes.title: Wing Whistle -- Second Mechanical Sound
nodes.description: Modified 9th primary feather produces high-pitched ascending whistle on takeoff. Functions as alarm signal -- triggers flight in nearby doves without any vocal call. Second mechanical sound in the engine (after Common Goldeneye at 124).

nodes.title: America's Most Hunted Game Bird
nodes.description: 20 million Mourning Doves killed annually. More than all duck species combined. Legal in 40 states. Washington opened hunting in 2019. Population remains stable due to extraordinary reproductive capacity (up to 6 broods/year).

nodes.title: Dual-Sound Species -- Vocal + Mechanical
nodes.description: The gentle coo and the sharp wing whistle. Two contrasting sounds define the species: soft mournful presence, then sudden percussive absence. Gentle and startling from the same bird.

cross_references.relationship: NATIVE CONTRAST PAIR! Band-tailed (forest, declining, hidden, 300-450g) vs. Mourning Dove (open, thriving, ubiquitous, 100-170g). Both native. Both E=3. Opposite ecology.
MECHANICAL SOUND LINK! Goldeneye wing whistle (124) was the first. Mourning Dove wing whistle (150) is the second. Both feather-generated. Rock Pigeon clap (152) will be the third.
NATIVE vs. INVASIVE TRANSITION! Mourning Dove (native, ancient, 300M) to Collared-Dove (invasive, 30 years, explosive). The boundary between native and non-native in the Columbidae.
MECHANICAL SOUND TRIO! Mourning Dove whistle (150) and Rock Pigeon clap (152): feather whistle vs. wing-body percussion. Both Columbidae. Different mechanisms.
